skill.name: brand-assets-setup
skill.description: Generate and install a complete brand asset pack for a Next.js App Router project, including favicons, PWA icons, social images, logos, metadata, and manifest setup.
skill.summary: Set up Next.js brand assets, metadata, and PWA icons from a generated asset folder.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the Ruby execution and weak-crypto hits are Markdown false positives from filenames, examples, and prose. The real risk is legitimate but elevated: the skill can use Bash, copy files, edit project metadata, create a manifest, and delete the supplied source folder after import.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Modification Capability
medium_findings.description: The skill is allowed to use Bash, Write, and Edit, then instructs the agent to copy generated assets, update layout metadata, and create or update public/manifest.json. This is expected for the workflow, but it can overwrite project files if paths are misdetected or the user supplies the wrong folder.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools and workflow explicitly permit project file writes and shell-backed copy operations. The behavior is legitimate for the skill, but the overwrite surface is clear.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Cleanup of User-Supplied Folder
medium_findings.description: The import phase tells the agent to delete the source folder after successful import. If the provided path is broad, mistaken, or contains files beyond generated assets, this can cause unintended data loss.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The deletion instruction is explicit and tied to a user-provided folder path. The skill does not include detailed path containment checks in the written instructions.

low_findings.title: Static Code-Execution Detections Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick execution findings come from Markdown inline code spans, file names, tables, and fenced examples. No Ruby code or executable backtick interpolation was found in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation lines containing Markdown code formatting and asset names. The repository files reviewed are Markdown, not Ruby source files.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Detections Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not correspond to hashing or encryption code in the reviewed files. The referenced lines are prose, metadata examples, image specifications, or URL examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic APIs, digests, or password storage logic. The scanner appears to match substrings inside ordinary Markdown text.

low_findings.title: External URL References Only
low_findings.description: The skill and README include external documentation links and an OG preview service. These are references for validation and learning, not automatic network requests or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs are present, but they are documentation or manual verification links. No evidence found of automatic HTTP calls or secret transmission.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash and File Write Tools Enabled
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash, Write, and Edit tools, which enable shell commands and project file mutation during normal use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool declaration is explicit in the skill front matter. This is a capability risk, not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Source Folder Deletion After Import
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes deleting the generated source folder after import, which should be constrained to a verified temporary asset folder.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deletion step is directly stated. Risk depends on path validation performed by the agent at runtime.

content.value_statement: Next.js teams often miss required favicon, PWA, social preview, and metadata files. This skill audits the project, produces exact asset specs, imports generated files, and wires metadata correctly.
actual_capabilities: Detects App Router layouts in app or src/app project structures.
Checks required favicon, app icon, PWA icon, social image, and logo files.
Extracts brand colors from CSS, Tailwind, or theme configuration files.
Prints exact generation specifications for a 12-file brand asset pack.
Copies generated assets into the correct Next.js locations.
Updates metadata and manifest settings for App Router conventions.
limitations: Designed for Next.js App Router projects, not Pages Router projects.
Requires the user to provide or generate the actual image assets.
Can overwrite existing assets when importing files into project paths.
Source folder cleanup depends on safe user-provided folder paths.

use_cases.title: Launch Brand Assets
use_cases.description: Prepare favicons, app icons, social cards, and manifest files before launching a new Next.js product.

use_cases.title: Standardize Client Deliverables
use_cases.description: Apply a repeatable checklist for brand asset placement, metadata, and PWA readiness across client sites.

use_cases.title: Repair Missing Metadata Assets
use_cases.description: Find missing or duplicated metadata assets and align App Router files with Next.js conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Brand Assets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to inspect my Next.js App Router project and show which brand asset files are missing or need updates.

prompt_templates.title: Create Asset Generation Specs
prompt_templates.prompt: Detect my brand colors and produce the complete 12-file asset checklist with exact sizes, backgrounds, and target paths.

prompt_templates.title: Import Generated Assets
prompt_templates.prompt: I generated the brand asset folder. Verify its contents, copy each file into the correct Next.js location, and update metadata safely.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Metadata and PWA Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my layout metadata, manifest, social preview assets, and next/image logo usage for App Router compatibility before release.

output_examples.input: Check a new App Router project before launch.
output: A checklist showing each required favicon, app icon, PWA icon, social image, and logo file.
Detected brand colors and notes for which files need solid or transparent backgrounds.
Next steps for generating and importing the missing assets.

output_examples.input: Install a generated brand asset folder.
output: A file-by-file import summary with source and destination paths.
Updated metadata guidance for manifest, Open Graph, Twitter, robots, and viewport fields.
Validation steps for browser favicon, social preview, PWA manifest, and Apple home screen icon.

best_practices: Review existing assets before allowing the skill to overwrite project files.
Use a dedicated generated asset folder that contains only the expected brand files.
Validate favicons, social previews, and manifest icons after deployment.
anti_patterns: Do not provide a broad folder path that contains unrelated project files.
Do not duplicate App Router auto-detected image files inside metadata fields.
Do not run the import step without checking generated image dimensions and formats.

faq.question: Does this skill generate images directly?
faq.answer: No. It prepares exact image specifications and imports assets after the user generates them.

faq.question: Which Next.js projects does it support?
faq.answer: It targets App Router projects that use app/layout.tsx or src/app/layout.tsx.

faq.question: Can it update existing metadata?
faq.answer: Yes. It can edit layout metadata for viewport, manifest, Open Graph, Twitter, and robots settings.

faq.question: Will it overwrite files?
faq.answer: It can overwrite brand asset files during import, so users should review paths before copying.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: No automatic network access is required. External links are used for manual validation and documentation.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The workflow is legitimate, but it uses shell and filesystem tools to copy, edit, and delete files.
